# Video games in North Korea

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

[![Two children, one sitting down and another one standing while aiming, are playing a light-gun shooter game in front of a large projected screen. It displays a forest area and appears to feature several animals which one must shoot to achieve points.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Childs_playing_shooting_in_video_games_DPRK.jpg/250px-Childs_playing_shooting_in_video_games_DPRK.jpg)](/wiki/File:Childs_playing_shooting_in_video_games_DPRK.jpg)

Children playing a [light-gun shooter](/wiki/Light-gun_shooter "Light-gun shooter") game in North Korea

[North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea") has a burgeoning [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") industry. North Koreans play video games on mobile phones, personal computers, home consoles, and in arcades. North Korea does develop its own video games, although foreign games are also popular among young people in the country as they are often distributed through *[jangmadang](/wiki/Jangmadang "Jangmadang")*, or North Korean [black markets](/wiki/Black_market "Black market").

Video games first appeared in the country during the 1990s in amusement arcades, and foreign cabinets are available in locations such as the [Pyongyang Gold Lane](/w/index.php?title=Pyongyang_Gold_Lane&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pyongyang Gold Lane (page does not exist)") and [Rungra People's Pleasure Ground](/wiki/Rungra_People%27s_Pleasure_Ground "Rungra People's Pleasure Ground"). Mobile games gained popularity in the 2010s with the success of a puzzle game called *Strength* (힘), leading to increased investment in the North Korean video game industry. Around 250 mobile games are available in North Korea. PC games are also popular among the country's youth, with foreign games often being distributed illegally on [USB flash drives](/wiki/USB_flash_drive "USB flash drive"). Foreign [home video game consoles](/wiki/Home_video_game_console "Home video game console") such as those produced by [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo"), [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony"), and [Subor](/wiki/Subor "Subor") are also available in North Korea.

## Arcades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_North_Korea&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Arcades")]

[![Two people are playing a racing game sitting on motorcycle-shaped seats connected to a pair of arcade cabinets labeled with the title "Cyber Cycles". Two other people stand aside and watch them play.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Cyber_Cycles_arcade_game_in_Pyongyang.jpg/250px-Cyber_Cycles_arcade_game_in_Pyongyang.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cyber_Cycles_arcade_game_in_Pyongyang.jpg)

A *[Cyber Cycles](/wiki/Cyber_Cycles "Cyber Cycles")* arcade cabinet at the [Pyongyang Gold Lane](/w/index.php?title=Pyongyang_Gold_Lane&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pyongyang Gold Lane (page does not exist)") bowling alley

[Amusement arcades](/wiki/Amusement_arcade "Amusement arcade") were the first way that video games became widely available to North Koreans, having first appeared in the country in the early 1990s. Arcades are common in the country.[[1]](#cite_note-YPT-2019-1) Popular arcade game genres include [light-gun shooter](/wiki/Light-gun_shooter "Light-gun shooter") games, [pinball](/wiki/Pinball "Pinball") machines,[[2]](#cite_note-Jia-2021-2) and [virtual reality games](/wiki/Virtual_reality_game "Virtual reality game").[[1]](#cite_note-YPT-2019-1) The [Pyongyang Gold Lane](/w/index.php?title=Pyongyang_Gold_Lane&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pyongyang Gold Lane (page does not exist)") is a [bowling alley](/wiki/Bowling_alley "Bowling alley") that has foreign arcade cabinets like *[Daytona USA](/wiki/Daytona_USA "Daytona USA")* available to play.[[2]](#cite_note-Jia-2021-2) The [Rungra People's Pleasure Ground](/wiki/Rungra_People%27s_Pleasure_Ground "Rungra People's Pleasure Ground") also has foreign arcade games such as *[Monaco GP](/wiki/Monaco_GP_(video_game) "Monaco GP (video game)")* and *[Let's Go Jungle](/wiki/Let%27s_Go_Jungle!:_Lost_on_the_Island_of_Spice "Let's Go Jungle!: Lost on the Island of Spice")*.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Mobile games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_North_Korea&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Mobile games")]

See also: [Telecommunications in North Korea](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_North_Korea "Telecommunications in North Korea")

[Mobile gaming](/wiki/Mobile_gaming "Mobile gaming") is popular in North Korea, and most people in [Pyongyang](/wiki/Pyongyang "Pyongyang") own smartphones.[[2]](#cite_note-Jia-2021-2) According to a 2021 estimate by the South Korean organization Korea Institute for National Unification, around 23% of North Koreans own a smartphone.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In large cities such as Pyongyang, there are physical stores that sell apps and games for people to install onto their phones.[[1]](#cite_note-YPT-2019-1) Smartphones in North Korea cannot access the Internet, but rather access the [Kwangmyong](/wiki/Kwangmyong_(network) "Kwangmyong (network)"), North Korea's [intranet](/wiki/Intranet "Intranet") service. North Korean phones run a modified version of an [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") operating system.[[2]](#cite_note-Jia-2021-2) The localized Android operating system used in North Korea restricts users from downloading unapproved software or viewing unsanctioned media.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[![An older man is sitting down on a train, staring down while playing a tile-matching mobile game. A child sitting next to him is also looking over at his phone.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Man_plays_Candy_Crush_knockoff_%2833139733395%29.jpg/250px-Man_plays_Candy_Crush_knockoff_%2833139733395%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Man_plays_Candy_Crush_knockoff_(33139733395).jpg)

A man playing a mobile game on the [Pyongyang Metro](/wiki/Pyongyang_Metro "Pyongyang Metro")

In 2014, a quiz game app called *Strength* (힘) that was developed at [Kim Il Sung University](/wiki/Kim_Il_Sung_University "Kim Il Sung University") reportedly sold 1 million copies. The success of the game caused increased investment in North Korean game development. In 2022, a South Korean research institute called the North Korea ICT Research Association (북한ICT연구회) estimated based on interviews with around 10 North Korean defectors that there were 250 mobile games available in a North Korean [app store](/wiki/App_store "App store") called "My Road Companion 4.3" (나의길동무 4.3).[[7]](#cite_note-Choe-2022-7)

North Korean mobile games are often [bootleg](/wiki/Bootleg_games "Bootleg games") versions of foreign games like *[Clash of Clans](/wiki/Clash_of_Clans "Clash of Clans")*,[[8]](#cite_note-8) *[Angry Birds](/wiki/Angry_Birds_(video_game) "Angry Birds (video game)")*, and *[Bejeweled](/wiki/Bejeweled_(video_game) "Bejeweled (video game)").*[[2]](#cite_note-Jia-2021-2) Domestically produced games also exist, such as a [strategy game](/wiki/Strategy_video_game "Strategy video game") called *Imjin Fatherland War 1.0* (임진조국전쟁 1.0),[[7]](#cite_note-Choe-2022-7) a [shooter game](/wiki/Shooter_game "Shooter game") called *Tiger Special Forces* (호랑이 특전대), and [educational games](/wiki/Educational_video_game "Educational video game") such as *Mysterious Dice* (신비한 주사위) and *School Trip* (수학여행).[[9]](#cite_note-9) [Nosotek](/wiki/Nosotek "Nosotek") is a Western-funded North Korean video game studio that has developed mobile games based on Western films like *[The Big Lebowski](/wiki/The_Big_Lebowski "The Big Lebowski")* and the *[Men in Black](/wiki/Men_in_Black_(film_series) "Men in Black (film series)")* series, which have been published in the West by [News Corp](/wiki/News_Corp "News Corp").[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The North Korean government has produced [propaganda](/wiki/Propaganda_in_North_Korea "Propaganda in North Korea") films against [problematic smartphone use](/wiki/Problematic_smartphone_use "Problematic smartphone use") and [video game addiction](/wiki/Video_game_addiction "Video game addiction").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## PC games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_North_Korea&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: PC games")]

According to a 2018 survey of 8,500 North Korean households conducted by [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF"), 18.7% of households owned a computer, and 1.4% of households could access the North Korean intranet.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Illegally acquired foreign video games are popular among urban youth. These games are typically shared on [USB flash drives](/wiki/USB_flash_drive "USB flash drive").[[14]](#cite_note-Vincent-2018-14) A North Korean defector who entered South Korea in 2019 claimed that North Korean police did not generally view the playing of foreign video games as a significant issue; they even viewed it as less serious than the consumption of foreign videos or music. They claimed that, if one were to be caught playing foreign video games, they could [bribe](/wiki/Bribery "Bribery") police officers to avoid harsher punishment.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The most popular PC video game is *[Counter-Strike](/wiki/Counter-Strike_(video_game) "Counter-Strike (video game)")*. Although online matches cannot be played, it is still popular to play against bots.[[1]](#cite_note-YPT-2019-1) Other popular PC games played in North Korea have included *[Grand Theft Auto V](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto_V "Grand Theft Auto V")*, *[FIFA Online](/wiki/FIFA_Online "FIFA Online"),*[[14]](#cite_note-Vincent-2018-14) *[Command & Conquer: Red Alert](/wiki/Command_%26_Conquer:_Red_Alert "Command & Conquer: Red Alert")*, *[Age of Empires](/wiki/Age_of_Empires "Age of Empires")*, *[DotA](/wiki/Defense_of_the_Ancients "Defense of the Ancients")*, and *[SimCity](/wiki/SimCity_(1989_video_game) "SimCity (1989 video game)")*.[[2]](#cite_note-Jia-2021-2) Because most North Koreans do not have access to personal computers, [computer cafés](/wiki/Internet_caf%C3%A9 "Internet café") were popular places for gaming in Pyongyang in the late 1990s.[[2]](#cite_note-Jia-2021-2) According to [Yonhap News Agency](/wiki/Yonhap_News_Agency "Yonhap News Agency"), computer cafés began to be granted permission to operate officially beginning on July 1, 2002. One such facility in [Chongjin](/wiki/Chongjin "Chongjin"), documented in 2005, required a monthly membership fee of 20,000 [North Korean won](/wiki/North_Korean_won "North Korean won") per month; by comparison, a typical North Korean person's salary was around 2,500 won per month. The business allowed access to the North Korean intranet and video games produced in North Korea and China.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The download statistics page of the video game storefront [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)") recorded only a single point of traffic from the entire country since 2013. *[PC Gamer](/wiki/PC_Gamer "PC Gamer")* suggested this to be "North Korea's only Steam user."[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In 2012, Nosotek was commissioned by [Koryo Tours](/wiki/Koryo_Tours "Koryo Tours") to create *[Pyongyang Racer](/wiki/Pyongyang_Racer "Pyongyang Racer")*, a browser-based [racing video game](/wiki/Racing_video_game "Racing video game") that is marketed towards Western tourists.[[18]](#cite_note-18) It was inspired by [retro](/wiki/Retro_gaming "Retro gaming") arcade racing games.[[19]](#cite_note-19) *Hunting Yankee* is a North Korean [first-person shooter](/wiki/First-person_shooter "First-person shooter") game about shooting American soldiers.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In 2025, the North Korean government began to develop a [computer gaming center](/wiki/LAN_gaming_center "LAN gaming center") in a facility with room for 300 PCs.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) In November 2025, images inside the gaming center were posted online and showed gaming stations equipped with high-end gaming PCs, monitors and headsets from the brand Republic of Gamers, which is a gaming PC brand of the Taiwanese company [Asus](/wiki/Asus "Asus"). Games available at the center include entries from franchises such as *[Resident Evil](/wiki/Resident_Evil "Resident Evil")*, *[Counter Strike](/wiki/Counter_Strike "Counter Strike")*, *[Dota](/wiki/Dota "Dota")*, *[Call of Duty](/wiki/Call_of_Duty "Call of Duty")*, *[Command & Conquer: Red Alert](/wiki/Command_%26_Conquer#Red_Alert_series "Command & Conquer")*, *[Warcraft](/wiki/Warcraft "Warcraft")*, *[Starcraft](/wiki/Starcraft "Starcraft")*, *[Ace Combat](/wiki/Ace_Combat "Ace Combat")*, *[The Last of Us](/wiki/The_Last_of_Us "The Last of Us")*, *[Crysis](/wiki/Crysis "Crysis")*, and *[Halo](/wiki/Halo_(franchise) "Halo (franchise)")*.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Home consoles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_North_Korea&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Home consoles")]

[![View of the PlayStation 1 console.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/9e/PlayStation-SCPH-1000-with-Controller.png/250px-PlayStation-SCPH-1000-with-Controller.png)](/wiki/File:PlayStation-SCPH-1000-with-Controller.png)

The [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation_(console) "PlayStation (console)") was available in North Korea in the 1990s, and was played by [Kim Jong Un](/wiki/Kim_Jong_Un "Kim Jong Un") in his youth.

Beginning in the 1990s, foreign [home video game consoles](/wiki/Home_video_game_console "Home video game console") like the [Famicom](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System") and [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation_(console) "PlayStation (console)") gained popularity in North Korea. Many were brought into the country by North Koreans traveling abroad,[[a]](#cite_note-27) and others were sent as gifts by [Zainichi Koreans](/wiki/Zainichi_Koreans "Zainichi Koreans") to their relatives in North Korea. They were also available in department stores, but were unaffordable for most North Koreans.[[2]](#cite_note-Jia-2021-2) Many games illegally imported for home consoles in the 1990s were played in temporary gaming centers operated in private homes to avoid government surveillance.[[7]](#cite_note-Choe-2022-7)

According to interviews with former classmates of [Kim Jong Un](/wiki/Kim_Jong_Un "Kim Jong Un") by journalist [Anna Fifield](/wiki/Anna_Fifield "Anna Fifield"), Kim Jong Un played basketball-themed [sports video games](/wiki/Sports_video_game "Sports video game") on the PlayStation when he lived in [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") in his youth.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

The Moranbong is a home video game console released on North Korean markets in 2019. The Moranbong is a localized version of the [Subor](/wiki/Subor "Subor") G80 (also known as the Cdragon Cassidy G80), a Chinese console that uses a [motion controller](/wiki/Motion_controller "Motion controller") like the [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii") and a camera like the [Kinect](/wiki/Kinect "Kinect"). It runs on the [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") operating system.[[28]](#cite_note-29) The Moranbong is sold pre-equipped with sports games, fitness games, educational games, and shooter games.[[7]](#cite_note-Choe-2022-7)

1. **[^](#cite_ref-27)** Since the [1990s North Korean famine](/wiki/1990s_North_Korean_famine "1990s North Korean famine"), it has not been uncommon for items to be smuggled across the northern border with China.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
